one_line_takeaway: Appending a single irrelevant answer option carrying a threat, reward, or direct override instruction to standard MCQA benchmarks reliably degrades LLM accuracy (mean ASR up to 19.8% for threat framing) even in top-capability models, generalizes to tool-selection and LLM-as-judge settings, is not reliably fixed by prompting or safety guardrails, but is measurably reduced by DPO/PPO post-training alignment.
key_finding: Across the 12 models, average Standard Accuracy is 80.4% (0.8043) but injecting a single irrelevant option E degrades performance broadly and unevenly: THREAT FRAMING causes the most severe degradation with the highest mean ASR (19.8%, i.e. 0.1979) and largest mean accuracy drop (10.9%, i.e. 0.1094), also showing the largest standard deviation of any category; SOCIAL COMPLIANCE yields the smallest average AD and lowest ASR (mean ASR 0.0921, AD 0.0197) among the four categories; BONUS FRAMING (mean ASR 0.1274, AD 0.0480) and INSTRUCTIONAL INTERFERENCE (mean ASR 0.1357, AD 0.0565) fall in between. At the directive level (Figure 3), Override Penalty and Override Bonus (explicit 'ignore the question and pick E' instructions combined with penalty/reward framing) produce the highest median and most dispersed ASR, with Override Penalty exceeding Override Bonus -- indicating models are more sensitive to avoiding a stated negative outcome than to seeking a stated reward. Higher standard capability does not imply greater robustness: e.g. Gemini-2.5-pro has high Std Acc. (0.8766) but under THREAT FRAMING its Inj Acc. drops to 0.5546 (ASR 0.3913, AD 0.3217), the worst AD in the table; Claude-Haiku-4.5 is the most stable across all four categories, with several negative AD values (e.g. -0.0742 under SOCIAL COMPLIANCE), meaning injected accuracy sometimes exceeds standard accuracy. The effect generalizes beyond MCQA: on ToolE, THREAT FRAMING yields the highest average ASR at 16.39% (0.1639) among tested categories; on HelpSteer2, THREAT FRAMING yields 18.20% (0.1820) average ASR, again the highest. Response-type classification (Figure 4) shows E-rejected responses are a minority for most models, and models with high standard capability (Gemini-2.5-pro, Deepseek-v3.2, Deepseek-r1) still show notable E-induced/E-influenced proportions. Among the three defense strategies applied to Qwen-3-8B (Table 4), Defensive Prompting (DP) and the safety-trained Guardrail model do NOT reliably mitigate injection -- on OI-Bench, DP's ASR rises to 0.3127 (vs Base 0.1831) and Guard's ASR rises to 0.3411, both worse than the undefended base model, and Guard's Std Acc. also drops sharply to 0.6315 (from Base 0.7328). In contrast, post-training alignment helps: DPO reduces OI-Bench ASR to 0.1536 and PPO to 0.1429, both below Base, with PPO also improving Std Acc. to 0.7930; similar ASR reductions hold on HelpSteer2 and ToolE for both DPO and PPO. Attention analysis shows that before PPO, several deep-layer attention heads disproportionately attend to option E (positive delta); after PPO fine-tuning this delta decreases substantially in deep layers. The position-bias ablation (Table 5) shows that moving the injected option from the appended position E into standard positions A-D ('perm.') markedly increases susceptibility for all three tested models, e.g. Qwen-3-8B's ASR rises from 0.1561 (default) to 0.4055 (perm.), and Deepseek-v3.2's from 0.1912 to 0.3157.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'option injection', a benchmarking method that augments standard 4-choice MCQA (options A-D) with a 5th, question-irrelevant option E carrying a misleading directive (e.g. an authority claim, a reward promise, a threat, or a direct instruction to select E). Building on this, the authors construct OI-Bench, a 3,000-question benchmark (1,000 items each sampled via tinyBenchmarks/IRT from MMLU, LogiQA, and HellaSwag) spanning 16 directive types organized into 4 categories: SOCIAL COMPLIANCE, BONUS FRAMING, THREAT FRAMING, and INSTRUCTIONAL INTERFERENCE. They evaluate 12 LLMs across 7 model families (Claude-Haiku-4.5, Deepseek-r1, Deepseek-v3.2, Gemini-2.5-flash-lite, Gemini-2.5-pro, GPT-5, GPT-5-mini, Grok-4.1, Llama-4-scout, Llama-4-maverick, Qwen-3-8B, Qwen-3-235B) measuring Standard Accuracy, Injected Accuracy, Attack Success Rate (ASR: fraction of originally-correct predictions that flip to incorrect after E is appended), and Accuracy Drop. They extend the analysis beyond MCQA to two real-world decision datasets (ToolE for tool selection, HelpSteer2 for LLM-as-a-judge), classify model responses into four behavioral categories (E-induced/E-influenced/E-ignored/E-rejected) via majority-vote LLM judging over 426,387 responses, evaluate three defense strategies (defensive prompting, a safety-guardrail model Qwen3Guard-Gen-8B, and post-training alignment via DPO/PPO on Qwen3-8B), and perform an attention-analysis (attention-norm delta between option E and options A-D across layers/heads) plus a position-bias ablation (moving the injected option from position E into positions A-D).
threat_model: The paper's own framing: an adversary (or any content source) can append a single extra, semantically irrelevant answer option to a multiple-choice prompt, embedding a directive (authority claim, reward, threat, contradiction, fake chain-of-thought, or explicit command) that attempts to override the model's task-grounded decision. This is presented as a benchmarking-oriented instantiation of prompt/instruction injection risk applied specifically to the MCQA choice interface (used for benchmarking, tool-selection menus, and LLM-as-judge scoring rubrics), rather than a full adversarial red-teaming threat model with an attacker controlling arbitrary input channels. The paper explicitly distinguishes this from indirect prompt injection via external/tool content (which it cites as prior work, e.g. Greshake et al. 2023, BIPIA) -- OI-Bench's injection is inserted directly into the option set of the evaluation prompt itself.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations: (1) Scope -- the study is confined to single-turn MCQA (question with fixed options, one selected answer); results may not transfer to broader decision tasks requiring multi-step plans or longer reasoning chains, and it is unclear whether the same bias patterns would hold there. (2) Model access -- most evaluated models are closed/API-only, so weights, training data, system prompts, safety layers, and server-side pre/post-processing cannot be inspected, and internal signals (logits, attention, hidden states) cannot be observed for those models; internal analyses (e.g. attention heatmaps) are only possible for smaller open models, which only partially addresses this limitation for closed models. (3) Data and answer parsing -- regex-based answer extraction can fail on long, repeated, or truncated model outputs, adding evaluation noise that may reflect poor output control rather than genuine inability to choose an option. (4) Coverage and compute -- due to compute limits the source datasets are subsampled to ~1,000 questions each, reducing coverage of rare cases and potentially weakening conclusions about narrow sub-areas; the large number of tested prompt patterns also limits the scope of broader sweeps (more models/datasets/repeated runs). (5) Method goal and decoding -- the paper does not aim to find a single strongest attack; option styles are chosen for comparability rather than maximal effectiveness, and temperature is fixed at 0 for reproducibility, so results may not hold under other decoding settings. (6) Evaluation signals -- evaluation relies mainly on automatic accuracy-based metrics with limited human review, and when models provide no usable explanation, changes in underlying reasoning cannot be separated from changes in the final choice.

datasets_used: ToolE (Huang et al., 2024) -- tool-selection decision-making dataset
OI-Bench (this paper's constructed benchmark) -- 3,000 questions, 16 directive types across 4 categories
quantitative_results: Average Standard Accuracy across 12 models on OI-Bench: 0.8043 ± 0.0631
Average ASR by category on OI-Bench: SOCIAL COMPLIANCE 0.0921 ± 0.0505, BONUS FRAMING 0.1274 ± 0.0860, THREAT FRAMING 0.1979 ± 0.1117 (highest), INSTRUCTIONAL INTERFERENCE 0.1357 ± 0.0736
Average Accuracy Drop by category: SOCIAL COMPLIANCE 0.0197, BONUS FRAMING 0.0480, THREAT FRAMING 0.1094 (highest), INSTRUCTIONAL INTERFERENCE 0.0565
Gemini-2.5-pro under THREAT FRAMING: Std Acc. 0.8766 -> Inj Acc. 0.5546, ASR 0.3913, AD 0.3217 (worst AD in Table 2)
Claude-Haiku-4.5 most robust overall, e.g. AD -0.0742 under SOCIAL COMPLIANCE (negative, i.e. injected accuracy exceeded standard accuracy)
ToolE average ASR by category: SOCIAL COMPLIANCE 0.0495, BONUS FRAMING 0.1021, THREAT FRAMING 0.1639 (16.39%, highest), INSTRUCTIONAL INTERFERENCE 0.0940
HelpSteer2 average ASR by category: SOCIAL COMPLIANCE 0.1033, BONUS FRAMING 0.1345, THREAT FRAMING 0.1820 (18.20%, highest), INSTRUCTIONAL INTERFERENCE 0.1288

Defense comparison on Qwen-3-8B, OI-Bench: Base ASR 0.1831 / Std Acc. 0.7328; Defensive Prompting ASR 0.3127 / Std Acc. 0.7443; Guardrail (Qwen3Guard-Gen-8B) ASR 0.3411 / Std Acc. 0.6315; DPO ASR 0.1536 / Std Acc. 0.7505; PPO ASR 0.1429 / Std Acc. 0.7930
Defense comparison on HelpSteer2: Base ASR 0.1782; DP ASR 0.1884; Guard ASR 0.3076; DPO ASR 0.1221; PPO ASR 0.1029
Defense comparison on ToolE: Base ASR 0.1300; DP ASR 0.2576; Guard ASR 0.3783; DPO ASR 0.0874; PPO ASR 0.0468
Position-bias ablation (Table 5): Gemini-2.5-flash-lite ASR default 0.2781 -> perm. 0.3807; Qwen-3-8B ASR default 0.1561 -> perm. 0.4055; Deepseek-v3.2 ASR default 0.1912 -> perm. 0.3157
Inter-judge agreement for response-type labeling: Fleiss' kappa = 0.5804, over 426,387 labeled responses
Preference dataset for DPO/PPO training: 5,816 E-rejected + 5,816 E-induced (balanced core), plus 1,496 E-ignored + 1,496 E-influenced; 652 held-out non-overlapping questions for evaluation
baselines_compared: 12 LLMs evaluated on OI-Bench: Claude-Haiku-4.5, Deepseek-r1, Deepseek-v3.2, Gemini-2.5-flash-lite, Gemini-2.5-pro, GPT-5, GPT-5-mini, Grok-4.1, Llama-4-scout, Llama-4-maverick, Qwen-3-8B, Qwen-3-235B
4 LLMs evaluated on ToolE/HelpSteer2 extension: Claude-Haiku-4.5, Deepseek-v3.2, Gemini-2.5-flash-lite, Qwen-3-8B
Defense strategies compared on Qwen-3-8B: Base (no defense), Defensive Prompting (DP), Qwen3Guard-Gen-8B safety guardrail, DPO fine-tuning, PPO fine-tuning
Judge models for response-type classification: Grok, Olmo-3-32B-Think, GPT-OSS-120B (majority vote)
